variables.f_in: Input AC frequency (Hz) — 50 Hz mains in India
variables.f_ripple: Ripple frequency (Hz) — f for half-wave, 2f for full-wave

locked_facts: A half-wave rectifier uses one diode to pass only the positive half-cycles of AC input; the output has a pulsating DC with frequency equal to input frequency f
A full-wave rectifier uses two diodes with a centre-tapped transformer (or four diodes in a bridge configuration) and rectifies both half-cycles; output ripple frequency is 2f
Average DC output: half-wave = V_peak/π ≈ 0.318 V_peak; full-wave = 2V_peak/π ≈ 0.636 V_peak
The ripple factor (ratio of AC component to DC component) is ~1.21 for half-wave and ~0.48 for full-wave — full-wave is much smoother
A capacitor filter connected across the load smooths the pulsating DC by charging during the peak and discharging slowly through the load between peaks
In a bridge rectifier, two diodes conduct during each half-cycle, but the current through the load always flows in the same direction
The diode voltage drop (~0.7 V for Si) reduces the output: V_out_peak = V_in_peak − 0.7 V (half-wave/centre-tap) or V_in_peak − 1.4 V (bridge, two diode drops)
truth_statements: A rectifier converts alternating current (AC) into pulsating direct current (DC) by allowing current to flow in only one direction using the unidirectional property of diodes
The full-wave rectifier is more efficient than the half-wave rectifier because it utilises both halves of the AC cycle, giving higher average output and lower ripple
A bridge rectifier achieves full-wave rectification without a centre-tapped transformer, using four diodes arranged in a bridge configuration
Adding a capacitor filter reduces ripple significantly: larger capacitance and smaller load current both lead to smoother DC output
The ripple frequency of a full-wave rectifier is twice the input frequency (100 Hz for 50 Hz input) because each half-cycle is rectified

aha_moment.what_to_show: Three waveforms stacked vertically: (1) input AC sine wave, (2) pulsating DC without filter showing gaps (half-wave) or continuous humps (full-wave), (3) smoothed DC with capacitor filter showing nearly flat line. A 'ripple' label with arrow shows how small the variation becomes with the filter. The capacitor charges during peaks and discharges slowly between peaks (animated charge/discharge).
aha_moment.visual_emphasis: The dramatic difference between pulsating DC (humpy waveform) and filtered DC (nearly flat line) with capacitor charge/discharge animation
aha_moment.why_this_breaks_misconception: Students see that a rectifier alone does NOT produce smooth DC — the capacitor filter is essential, and its charge-discharge cycle is what creates the smoothing

simulation_states.label: AC input and half-wave rectifier
simulation_states.description: Left panel: a simple circuit with AC source, one diode, and a load resistor. During the positive half-cycle, the diode is forward biased and current flows (shown with animated current dots). During the negative half-cycle, the diode is reverse biased and no current flows. Right panel: input sine wave (blue) and output waveform (green) plotted — output shows only positive half-cycles with gaps during negative half-cycles.
simulation_states.emphasis: Half-wave: only positive half-cycles pass through — the negative half is completely blocked by the diode

simulation_states.label: Full-wave rectifier (centre-tap and bridge)
simulation_states.description: Two circuit variants shown. Top: centre-tapped transformer with two diodes — D1 conducts during positive half, D2 during negative half. Bottom: bridge rectifier with four diodes. In both, current through load always flows in the same direction. Right panel: input (blue) and output (green) — output shows continuous positive humps with no gaps. Average output = 2V_peak/π. Ripple frequency = 2f labeled.
simulation_states.emphasis: Full-wave rectifies BOTH halves — no gaps in output, double the average DC, ripple frequency doubles to 2f

simulation_states.label: Capacitor filter smoothing
simulation_states.description: A capacitor is added across the load in the full-wave rectifier circuit. The right panel shows three waveforms: (1) AC input, (2) unfiltered pulsating DC, (3) filtered nearly-smooth DC. The capacitor charges to the peak during diode conduction and discharges slowly through the load between peaks (animated). Ripple voltage magnitude shown decreasing as capacitance slider increases.
simulation_states.emphasis: The capacitor charges at peaks and slowly discharges — larger C means less ripple and smoother DC output

simulation_states.label: Interactive rectifier lab
simulation_states.description: Student selects rectifier type (half-wave, full-wave centre-tap, bridge) and adjusts input voltage amplitude, frequency, and filter capacitance. Left panel shows the live circuit with animated current flow. Right panel shows real-time input and output waveforms. Ripple factor, average DC voltage, and efficiency values update continuously. Increasing capacitance visibly flattens the output waveform.
simulation_states.emphasis: Compare half-wave vs full-wave, explore how capacitor value affects ripple — build intuition for practical power supplies

common_misconceptions.belief: A rectifier directly produces smooth DC like a battery
trigger_phrases: rectifier se seedha DC aata hai
common_misconceptions.correction: A rectifier alone produces pulsating DC, not smooth DC. The output has significant ripple (voltage fluctuations). A filter circuit (typically a capacitor) is needed to smooth the output. Even with a filter, some ripple remains — it is never as perfectly flat as a battery.
common_misconceptions.simulation_emphasis: Show raw rectifier output (humpy waveform) alongside a battery's flat line — then add capacitor filter to show gradual smoothing

common_misconceptions.belief: In a full-wave rectifier, the negative half-cycle is removed just like in half-wave
trigger_phrases: full wave mein bhi negative hatata hai
common_misconceptions.correction: A full-wave rectifier does NOT remove the negative half-cycle. Instead, it flips (inverts) the negative half-cycle to make it positive. This is why the output has no gaps — both halves of the input contribute to the output, just with the same polarity.
common_misconceptions.simulation_emphasis: Animate the negative half-cycle being flipped upward (not deleted) — show input negative half in red becoming positive output in green

common_misconceptions.belief: More diodes in a bridge rectifier means more voltage loss and lower efficiency than centre-tap
common_misconceptions.correction: While a bridge rectifier does have two diode drops (1.4 V total) compared to one (0.7 V) in a centre-tap rectifier, the bridge does not require an expensive centre-tapped transformer and can use the full secondary voltage. For most practical applications, bridge rectifiers are preferred despite the extra diode drop.
common_misconceptions.simulation_emphasis: Compare: centre-tap needs V_secondary/2 per half but only 0.7 V drop vs bridge uses full V_secondary but 1.4 V drop — show net output is similar

legend.meaning: Peak (maximum) value of AC input voltage (V)
